topStory.title: Show HN: Agent-desktop – Native desktop automation CLI for AI agents

stories.title: Show HN: Agent-desktop – Native desktop automation CLI for AI agents
stories.storyText: I've been building computer-use tools for a while, and I quietly launched this about a month ago (122 Stars on GH). I figured it was worth sharing here. Over the last few months, a lot of computer-use agents have come out: Codex, Claude Code, CUA, and others. Most of them seem to work roughly like this: 1. Take a screenshot 2. Have the model predict pixel coordinates 3. Click x,y 4. Take another screenshot 5. Repeat That works, but it's slow, expensive in tokens, and fragile. If the UI shifts a

Regression: malware reminder on every read still causes subagent refusals
stories.storyText: Not sure if anybody else has experienced this, but for my job I've been playing around with Claude Managed Agents to run code generation tasks in our repo. Every read operation in the managed agent is appended with a system prompt instructing Claude to scan the file for malware; Claude then wastes a bunch of time and tokens (money) performing the analysis; then, once the agent has confirmed that it is not malware, it still interprets the appended prompt to mean that it is disallowed to augment o

topStory.title: Show HN: Drive any macOS app in the background without stealing the cursor

stories.title: Show HN: Drive any macOS app in the background without stealing the cursor
stories.storyText: Hi HN, Francesco from Cua here. I hacked this project together last weekend, inspired by the Codex Computer-Use release and lessons learned from deploying GUI-operating agents for our customers. The main problem: when a UI automation process controls a desktop app today, it usually takes over the human’s session. Your cursor moves, keyboard focus gets stolen, windows jump to the front, and you have to stop working until the agent is done. That is why we have historically avoided encouraging user

topStory.title: Show HN: OSS Agent I built topped the TerminalBench on Gemini-3-flash-preview

stories.title: Show HN: OSS Agent I built topped the TerminalBench on Gemini-3-flash-preview
stories.storyText: Scored 65.2% vs google's official 47.8%, and the existing top closed source model Junie CLI's 64.3%. Since there are a lot of reports of deliberate cheating on TerminalBench 2.0 lately ( https://debugml.github.io/cheating-agents/ ), I would like to also clarify a few things 1. Absolutely no {agents/skills}.md files were inserted at any point. No cheating mechanisms whatsoever 2. The cli agent was run in leaderboard compliant way (no modification of resources or timeouts) 3. The full terminal ben

topStory.title: Show HN: A terminal spreadsheet editor with Vim keybindings

stories.title: Show HN: A terminal spreadsheet editor with Vim keybindings
stories.storyText: While speccing out this spreadsheet tool, I realized that I never had to think about the keybindings. It all just came naturally from Vim. Normal/insert/visual modes, hjkl navigation, dd/yy/p, :w, :q. The usual muscle memory works. It supports CSV/TSV import and export, and a native .cell format that preserves formulas. The formula engine handles SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MIN, MAX, and IF with range references. The codebase is a Cargo workspace: a pure cell-sheet-core library (no TUI dependency) and
